policy_statement: By 2021, develop guidelines on climate change effects on human mobility in Chile aligned with international instruments (A8a).
mechanism_description: Developing guidelines on climate change impacts on human mobility supports planning and coordination to address climate-related displacement and migration risks.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard risk → planning/coordination guidance → ↓ exposure & ↓ vulnerability (displacement/migration impacts)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human/system activity: Governments and agencies create and apply formal guidelines/standards for assessing and managing climate change impacts on human mobility (displacement, relocation, migration) and coordinating across sectors and jurisdictions.  
2) Mediating process: Better risk assessment, early planning, defined roles/protocols, and integration with international frameworks improves preparedness, service continuity, and managed movement/settlement under climate stressors.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced adverse impacts from climate hazards on affected populations (lower vulnerability, improved adaptive capacity, reduced disruption and humanitarian risk associated with climate-driven mobility).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved preparedness and coordinated response that reduces vulnerability/exposure of people and systems to climate-related displacement and migration impacts.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Climate hazards increase displacement/migration risk; guidelines improve planning and coordination, which primarily reduces exposure and vulnerability—an adaptation outcome.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While governance guidance could indirectly affect emissions (e.g., through settlement patterns), the direct, intended, and largest climate effect is managing climate impacts on people and systems, not GHG mitigation or resource efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: If guidelines explicitly mandate low-carbon relocation/settlement or transport decarbonization, mitigation co-benefits could occur, but they are not the dominant mechanism implied by “impacts on human mobility” guidance.  
4) Primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is developing climate–human mobility guidelines is fundamentally about preparedness/response for climate-driven mobility, which is adaptation.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any secondary tagging (e.g., migration management, disaster risk reduction, social protection) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Guidelines could be framed as general governance capacity-building; if they are purely informational with no link to implementation, adaptation benefits depend on uptake. Potential mitigation co-benefits via planned settlement/transport choices are secondary unless explicitly central.
key_indicators: “By 2021, guidelines… will be developed”; “effect of climate change”; “phenomenon of human mobility”; “in line with related international instruments”
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a non-binding standard (guidance), but absence of compliance language (“shall/mandatory”) and emphasis on development/alignment indicates a planning/strategy instrument rather than a regulatory mandate or performance standard.
